reference_research_study_list.title: Imagenet: A large-scale hierarchical image database.
Davit: Dual attention vision transformers.
reference_research_study_list.abstract:
In this work, we introduce Dual Attention Vision Transformers (DaViT), a
simple yet effective vision transformer architecture that is able to capture
global context while maintaining computational efficiency. We propose
approaching the problem from an orthogonal angle: exploiting self-attention
mechanisms with both "spatial tokens" and "channel tokens". With spatial
tokens, the spatial dimension defines the token scope, and the channel
dimension defines the token feature dimension. With channel tokens, we have the
inverse: the channel dimension defines the token scope, and the spatial
dimension defines the token feature dimension. We further group tokens along
the sequence direction for both spatial and channel tokens to maintain the
linear complexity of the entire model. We show that these two self-attentions
complement each other: (i) since each channel token contains an abstract
representation of the entire image, the channel attention naturally captures
global interactions and representations by taking all spatial positions into
account when computing attention scores between channels; (ii) the spatial
attention refines the local representations by performing fine-grained
interactions across spatial locations, which in turn helps the global
information modeling in channel attention. Extensive experiments show our DaViT
achieves state-of-the-art performance on four different tasks with efficient
computations. Without extra data, DaViT-Tiny, DaViT-Small, and DaViT-Base
achieve 82.8%, 84.2%, and 84.6% top-1 accuracy on ImageNet-1K with 28.3M,
49.7M, and 87.9M parameters, respectively. When we further scale up DaViT with
1.5B weakly supervised image and text pairs, DaViT-Gaint reaches 90.4% top-1
accuracy on ImageNet-1K. Code is available at https://github.com/dingmyu/davit.
reference_research_study_list.full_text: DaViT: Dual Attention Vision Transformers Mingyu Ding1, Bin Xiao2⋆, Noel Codella2, Ping Luo1⋆, Jingdong Wang3, Lu Yuan2 1The University of Hong Kong 2Microsoft Cloud + AI 3Baidu mingyuding@hku.hk {bixi,ncodella,luyuan}@microsoft.com pluo@cs.hku.hk wangjingdong@outlook.com Abstract. In this work, we introduce Dual Attention Vision Transform- ers (DaViT), a simple yet effective vision transformer architecture that is able to capture global context while maintaining computational ef- ficiency. We propose approaching the problem from an orthogonal an- gle: exploiting self-attention mechanisms with both “spatial tokens”and “channel tokens”. With spatial tokens, the spatial dimension defines the token scope, and the channel dimension defines the token feature dimen- sion. With channel tokens, we have the inverse: the channel dimension defines the token scope, and the spatial dimension defines the token fea- ture dimension. We further group tokens along the sequence direction for both spatial and channel tokens to maintain the linear complexity of the entire model. We show that these two self-attentions complement each other: (i) since each channel token contains an abstract representa- tion of the entire image, the channel attention naturally captures global interactions and representations by taking all spatial positions into ac- count when computing attention scores between channels; (ii) the spatial attention refines the local representations by performing fine-grained in- teractions across spatial locations, which in turn helps the global infor- mation modeling in channel attention. Extensive experiments show our DaViT achieves state-of-the-art performance on four different tasks with efficient computations. Without extra data, DaViT-Tiny, DaViT-Small, and DaViT-Base achieve 82.8%, 84.2%, and 84.6% top-1 accuracy on ImageNet-1K with 28.3M, 49.7M, and 87.9M parameters, respectively. When we further scale up DaViT with 1.5B weakly supervised image and text pairs, DaViT-Gaint reaches 90.4% top-1 accuracy on ImageNet-1K. Code is available at https://github.com/dingmyu/davit. 1 Introduction The global context is essential for many computer vision approaches, such as image classification and semantic segmentation. Convolutional neural networks (CNNs) [41] gradually obtain a global receptive field by multi-layer architec- tures and down-sampling operators. Recently, vision transformers [7,18], which directly capture long-range visual dependencies with a single self-attention layer, have drawn much attention. While these methods present strong capabilities to model the global context, their computational complexity grows quadratically with the token length, limiting its ability to scale up to high-resolution scenarios. ⋆ Corresponding author2 M. Ding et al. spatial dimensionspatial dimension(a) Spatial Window MultiheadSelf-attention (b) Channel Group Self-attention transposetokenization spatialtoken  channel token Fig. 1.(a) Spatial window multihead self-attention splits the spatial dimension into local windows, where each window contains multiple spatial tokens. Each token is also divided into multiple heads. (b) Channel group single-head self-attention groups channel tokens into multi groups. Attention is performed in each channel group with an entire image-level channel as a token. A channel-wise token that captures global information is also highlighted in (a). In this work, we alternately use these two types of attention to obtain both local fine-grained and global features. Designing an architecture that can capture global contexts while maintaining efficiency to learn from high-resolution inputs is still an open research problem. A substantial body of work has been dedicated to developing vision transform- ers toward this goal. iGPT [7] first utilized a standard transformer to solve vision tasks by treating the image as sequences of pixels and performing pixel- level interactions. After that, ViT [18] used non-overlapped image patches as tokens to model the relationship between small image patches instead of pixels, showing promising performance on middle-resolution tasks such as classifica- tion. To further reduce the computational cost, local attention [40,82,58] that limits attention in a spatially local window, and squeezed projection [65,62] that performs attention on downsampled tokens, were proposed. Though local atten- tion methods benefit from linear complexity with the spatial size, operators like “Shift” [40], “Overlapping Patch” [65,62], “ConvFFN” [62,79,68] are indispens- able to compensate for the loss of global contextual information. The general pattern across all prior works is that they attain various tradeoffs between resolution, global context, and computational complexity: pixel-level [7] and patch-level [18,40,65,63] self-attentions suffer either the cost of quadratic computational overhead or loss of global contextual information. Beyond varia- tions of pixel-level and patch-level self-attentions, can we design an image-level self-attention mechanism that captures global information but is still efficient concerning the spatial size? In this work, we introduce such a self-attention mechanism that is able to capture global context while maintaining computational efficiency. In addition to “spatial tokens”defined by existing works in Fig 1(a) representing the feature of an image patch, we introduce “ channel tokens” by applying self-attention to the transpose of the token matrix, as shown in Fig 1(b). With channel tokens, the channel dimension defines the token scope, and the spatial dimension de- fines the token feature dimension. In this way, each channel token is global on the spatial dimension, containing an abstract representation of the entire im- age. Correspondingly, performing self-attention on such channel tokens further captures the global interaction by taking all spatial positions into account whenDaViT: Dual Attention Vision Transformers 3 4 6 8 10 12 14 16 FLOPs (G) 81 82 83 84 85Acc (%) Image Classification Swin-Tiny/Small/Base Twins-SVT-Small/Base/Large Focal-Tiny/Small/Base Dual-Tiny/Small/Base(Ours) 900 1000 1100 1200 1300 FLOPs (G) 44 45 46 47 48 49 50mIoU (%) Semantic Segmentation Swin-Tiny/Small/Base Twins-SVT-Small/Base/Large Focal-Tiny/Small/Base Dual-Tiny/Small/Base(Ours) 250 300 350 400 450 500 FLOPs (G) 41 42 43 44 45 46 47 48 49mAP (%) Object Detection Swin-Tiny/Small/Base Twins-SVT-Small/Base Focal-Tiny/Small/Base Dual-Tiny/Small/Base(Ours) 250 300 350 400 450 500 FLOPs (G) 39 40 41 42 43 44 45mAP (%) Instance Segmentation Swin-Tiny/Small/Base Twins-SVT-Small/Base Focal-Tiny/Small/Base Dual-Tiny/Small/Base(Ours) Fig. 2.Comparisons of the efficiency (i.e., FLOPs) and performance (e.g., Acc, mIoU, mAP) between the proposed approach and existing SoTA methods [40,72,10] on four computer vision tasks. Each method is represented by a circle, whose size represents the number of parameters. Our approach achieves superior performance under similar FLOPs than its counterparts on all four benchmarks. computing attention scores between channels. Compared to conventional self- attention that performs global interactions over local pixels or patch tokens at a quadratic computational cost, the information exchange of channel self-attention is naturally imposed from a global perspective rather than a pixel/patch-wise one. Based on the global receptive field of the channel token, it fuses the rep- resentations to produce new global tokens and passes the information to the following layers. Thus, one can take such channel self-attention as a dynamic feature fusion over a series of abstract representations of the entire image. Although this approach presents many advantages, a few challenges must be overcome. First, the computational complexity suddenly increases quadratically with the channel dimension, limiting the representation power of the layers. Inspired by spatial local attention [40,82,58], we propose channel group attention by dividing the feature channels into several groups and performing image-level interactions within each group. By group attention, we reduce the complexity to linear with respect to both the spatial and the channel dimensions. Since each channel token contains an abstract representation of the entire image, self- attention in this setting naturally captures the global interaction even we apply it locally along the channel dimensions. Second, though channel-wise self-attention can capture global information easily, image-level tokens hinder local interactions across spatial locations. To solve this problem, we introduce Dual Attention Vision Transformers (DaViT) that alternately applies spatial window attention and channel group attention to capture both short-range and long-range visual dependencies, as shown in Fig- ure 1. Our results show that these two structures complement each other: the channel group attention provides a global receptive field on the spatial dimension and extracts high-level global-image representations by dynamic feature fusion across global channel tokens; the spatial window attention refines the local repre- sentations by performing fine-grained local interactions across spatial locations, which in turn helps the global information modeling in channel attention. To summarize, we propose DaViT that contains two seamlessly integrated self-attentions: spatial window self-attention with “spatial tokens”, and channel group self-attention with “channel tokens”. The two forms of attention are com- plementary and alternatively arranged, providing both local fine-grained and4 M. Ding et al. global interactions in a computationally efficient manner. We evaluate the ef- fectiveness of the proposed DaViT via comprehensive empirical studies on im- age classification, object detection, and segmentation. Results in Figure 2 show that DaViT consistently outperforms the SoTA vision transformers across three benchmarks and four tasks with even fewer computational costs. 2 Related Work Vision Transformers and MLPs. Transformers [59,15] have dominated a wide range of natural language processing tasks. In computer vision, pioneering works iGPT [7] and ViT [18] apply attention directly to a sequence of image pixels or patches. Similarly, follow-up works [25,55,63,51,66,20,80,46,47] model global relationships on the patch-level tokens. All these works apply attention to capture interactions over all the local tokens, which can be pixel-level or patch-level. In this work, we model the global relationship from an orthogonal perspective and apply attention mechanisms to both spatial tokens as well as their transpose, which we refer to as image-level (global) channel tokens. In this manner, we capture both fine-grained structural patterns and global interactions. Most recently, MLP-based models [53,54,39,74,8,35,27] draw attention by us- ing multi-layer perceptrons (MLPs) directly over patch features. MLP-Mixer [53] leverages feature transpose by applying MLPs across either spatial locations or feature channels repeatedly. However, it resorts to neither transformer architec- tures nor self-attention layers, and works only on the image classification task. Our work focuses on transformer-based architectures to benefit various tasks, e.g., classification, objection detection, and segmentation. Hierarchical Vision Transformers. Hierarchical designs are widely adopted to transformers [40,63,65,16,82,58,43,76,34,1,32,84,52,58,9,31,73,29,71,33,85] in vision. PVT [63] and CvT [65] perform attention on the squeezed tokens to re- duce the computational cost. Swin Transformer [40], ViL [82], and HaloNet [58] apply local windows attention to the patch tokens, which capture fine-grained features and reduce the quadratic complexity to linear, but lose the ability of global modeling. To compensate for the loss of global context, Swin Trans- former [40] conducts attention on the shifted local windows alternatively between consecutive blocks, and ViL [82] and HaloNet [58] play on overlapped windows. In this work, the proposed approach shares merits of hierarchical architec- tures and fine-grained local attention, meanwhile our proposed group channel attention still efficiently models the global context. Channel-wise Attentions. The author of [28] first proposes a Squeeze-and- Excitation (SE) block as channel-wise attention to re-calibrating the channel- wise features through the squeezed global feature. Other operators in CNNs related to our work are Dynamic Head [12] and DANet [19]. They apply attention along different feature dimensions on top of the CNN backbone for a specific task. Some transformer architectures involve channel-wise operations as well to reduce the computational costs. It contains two transformer blocks: spatial window self-attention and channel group self-attention blocks. By alter- nately using the two types of attention, our model enjoys the benefit of capturing both local fine-grained and global image-level interactions. XCiT [1] proposes cross-covariance attention (XCA) for efficient processing of high-resolution images. Similarly, CoaT [70] introduces a factorized attention mechanism that works efficiently in a multi-branch transformer backbone. Our work proposes channel group attention to capture global information in transformers. Though it has only linear complexity with respect to both channel and spatial dimensions, we demonstrate its power when combined with spa- tial window attention, forming our dual attention mechanism. Furthermore, we analyze in detail how our dual attention obtains global interactions as well as fine-grained local features, showing its effectiveness in benefiting various tasks, e.g., classification, objection detection, and segmentation. 3 Methodology We propose Dual Attention Vision Transformers (DaViT), a clean, efficient, yet effective transformer backbone containing both local fine-grained features and global representations. In this section, we first introduce the hierarchical layout of our model. We then detail our channel group attention and the combination with spatial window attention [40]. 3.1 Overview We divide the model into four stages, where a patch embedding layer is inserted at the beginning of each stage. We stack our dual attention blocks in each stage with the resolution and feature dimension kept the same. Figure 3(a) illustrates the architecture of our dual attention block, consisting of a spatial window at- tention block and a channel group attention block.6 M. Ding et al. Preliminaries. Let us assume a RP×C dimensional visual feature, where P is the number of total patches and C is the number of total channels. Simply applying the standard global self-attention leads to a complexity of O(2P2C + 4PC 2). It is defined as: A(Q, K, V) = Concat(head1, . . . ,headNh ) where head i = Attention(Qi, Ki, Vi) = softmax Qi(Ki)T √Ch  Vi (1) where Qi = XiWQ i , Ki = XiWK i , and Vi = XiWV i are RP×Ch dimensional visual features with Nh heads, Xi denotes the ith head of the input feature and Wi denotes the projection weights of the ith head for Q, K, V, and C = Ch ∗Nh. Please note that the output projection WO is omitted here. Considering P can be very large, e.g., 128 × 128, the computational cost is immoderate. In DaViT, we alternatively arrange spatial window attention and channel group attention to obtain both local and global features, but with a linear com- plexity to the spatial dimension, as shown in Figure 3. 3.2 Spatial Window Attention Window attention computes self-attention within local windows, as shown in Fig- ure 1(a). The windows are arranged to partition the image in a non-overlapping manner evenly. Supposing there are Nw different windows with each window containing Pw patches, where P = Pw ∗ Nw. Then window attention can be represented by: Awindow(Q, K, V) = {A(Qi, Ki, Vi)}Nw i=0 (2) where Qi, Ki, Vi ∈ RPw×Ch are local window queries, keys, and values. The computational complexity of a window-based self-attention isO(2PPwC+4PC 2) with a linear complexity with the spatial sizeP. More details of window attention are shown in Figure 3(b). Though the computation is reduced, window attention loses the ability to model the global information. We will show that our proposed channel attention naturally solves this problem and mutually benefits with window attention. 3.3 Channel Group Attention We visit self-attention from another perspective and propose channel-wise at- tention, as shown in Figure 3(c). Previous self-attentions [59,40,65,58,81,7] in vision define tokens with pixels or patches, and gather the information along spatial dimensions. Instead of performing attention on pixel-level or patch-level, we apply attention mechanisms on the transpose of patch-level tokens. To obtain global information in the spatial dimension, we set the number of heads equal to 1. We argue that each transposed token abstracts the global information. In thisDaViT: Dual Attention Vision Transformers 7 way, channel tokens interact with global information on the channel dimension in linear spatial-wise complexity, as shown in Figure 1(b). Simply transposing the feature can obtain a vanilla channel-level attention with a complexity of O(6PC 2). To further reduce the computational complexity, we group channels into multiple groups and perform self-attention within each group. Formally, letNg denotes the number of groups andCg denotes the number of channels in each group, we have C = Ng ∗ Cg. In this way, our channel group attention is global, with image-level tokens interacting across a group of channels. It is defined as: Achannel(Q, K, V) = {Agroup(Qi, Ki, Vi)T }Ng i=0 Agroup(Qi, Ki, Vi) = softmax " QT i Kip Cg # VT i (3) where Qi, Ki, Vi ∈ RP×Cg are grouped channel-wise image-level queries, keys, and values. Note that although we transpose the tokens in channel attention, the projection layers W and the scaling factor 1√ Cg remain performed and computed along the channel dimension, rather than the spatial one. Considering that the number of spatial patches varies with the image size, the above design ensures our model can generalize to any image size. Complexity analysis. Our channel group attention is performed on the image- level tokens across the channel dimension. Compared to window attention that produces an attention map with size Pw × Pw, the channel-wise attention map is of Cg × Cg-dimensional. The overall computational complexity of our model includes O(2PC (Pw + Cg)) for window and channel attentions, O(8PC 2) for linear projections, and O(16PC 2) for FFNs (expand ratio is 4). It can be seen that our dual attention is computationally efficient with linear complexity to both the spatial and channel dimensions. FFN dominates the number of FLOPs and model parameters. Considering our dual attention has both channel-wise and spatial-wise interactions, in this work, we conduct an initial exploration to show the potential of the pure-attention structure without FFNs. Details can be found in Appendix. Global interactions in channel attention. Channel attention naturally cap- tures global information and interactions for visual recognition tasks. (i) After transposing the feature, each channel token itself is global on the spatial dimen- sion, providing a global view of the image. (ii) Given Cg tokens with dimension P, the Cg × Cg-dimensional attention map is computed by involving all spatial locations, i.e., (Cg ×P) ·(P ×Cg). (iii) With such a global attentive map, chan- nel attention fuses multiple global views of the image dynamically, producing new global tokens and passing the information to the following spatial-wise lay- ers. Compared to spatial-wise global attentions [55,18] that perform interactions across spatial locations, the information exchange of our channel self-attention is imposed from a global perspective rather than a patch-wise one, complementing the spatial window attention. Detailed analysis can be found in Sec. 4.8 M. Ding et al. Original image Output featuremap Input featuremaps before the channel attention (top-7 channels attending to the output channel, sorted by attention score) Fig. 4.Illustrating how channel attention gathering global information by visualizing attended feature maps. The first and second columns denote the original image and a feature channel after our channel attention; while the other columns are the channel tokens with top-7 highest attention scores before the attention. Channel attention is able to select globally important regions and suppress background regions for better recognition. The third network stage of the classification model is used for visualization. 3.4 Model Instantiation In this work, we follow the design strategy suggested by previous works [40,72]. Take an image with H ×W, a C-dimensional feature with a resolution of H 4 × W 4 is obtained after the first patch embedding layer. And its resolution is further reduced into H 8 × W 8 , H 16 × W 16 , and H 32 × W 32 with the feature dimension increasing to 2 C, 4 C, and 8 C after the other three patch embedding layer, respectively. Here, our patch embedding layer is implemented by stride convolution. The convolutional kernels and stride values of our four patch embedding layers are {7, 2, 2, 2} and {4, 2, 2, 2}, respectively. We consider three different network configurations for image classification, objection detection, and segmentation: – DaViT-Tiny: C = 96, L= {1,1,3,1}, Ng = Nh = {3,6,12,24} – DaViT-Small: C = 96, L= {1,1,9,1}, Ng = Nh = {3,6,12,24} – DaViT-Base: C = 128, L= {1,1,9,1}, Ng = Nh = {4,8,16,32}, where L is the layer numbers, Ng is the number of groups in channel attention, and Nh is the number of heads in window attention for each stage. When more training data involved, we further scale up DaViT to large, huge, and giant size to validate the scaling ability of the proposed architecture for image classification: – DaViT-Large: C = 192, L= {1,1,9,1}, Ng = Nh = {6,12,24,48} – DaViT-Huge: C = 256, L= {1,1,9,1}, Ng = Nh = {8,16,32,64} – DaViT-Giant: C = 384, L= {1,1,12,3}, Ng = Nh = {12,24,48,96}. See Appendix for more details of model configurations. 4 Analysis Interpretation of global interactions. Global interactions in transformers can be summarized into different types. Vanilla ViT [18] and DeiT [55] performDaViT: Dual Attention Vision Transformers 9 DaViT Swin DeiT DaViT Swin DeiT DaViT Swin DeiT Stage 1 Stage 2 Stage 3 Stage 4 Fig. 5.Feature map visualization of our DaViT, Swin [40], and DeiT [55] at four different network stages (from top to bottom representing stages 1 to 4). For all network stages, a random feature channel is visualized. information exchange between different patches among the whole image; Focal Transformer [72] proposes interactions between tokens with different scales to get a larger receptive field; PVT [62] leverages a spatial reduction mechanism to obtain a coarse approximation of global attention; Swin [40] stacks multiple hierarchical layers to get global information eventually. Unlike them, a single block with our channel attention is able to learn the global interactions from another perspective by taking all spatial positions into account when computing the attention scores, as in ( Cg × P) · (P × Cg). It captures the information from multiple global tokens, which represent different abstract views of the entire image. For example, different channels may contain information from different parts of an object; such part information can be aggregated into a global view. Figure 4 illustrates how our channel attention works by visualizing the fea- turemaps before and after the attention. For each image in the first column, we randomly choose an output channel (the second column) in the third stage of the network and its corresponding top-7 relevant input channels for visual- ization. We can see that the channel attention fuses information from multiple tokens, selects globally important regions, and suppresses unimportant regions. Relation to Swin and DeiT. To show the effectiveness of our dual self- attention, we make detailed comparisons with two representative clean baselines: Swin [40] with fine-grained local features, and DeiT [55] with global coarse fea- tures. Note that though our work and Swin both use window attention as a network element, the key design of Swin, i.e., the use of “shifted window parti- tions” between successive layers to increase the receptive field, is not utilized in our work. We also simplifies its relative position encoding [11] with a depth-wise convolution before layernorm to get a cleaner structure for arbitrary input sizes. We randomly visualize a feature channel in each stage of DaViT, Swin [40], and DeiT [55]. We observe that: (i) Swin captures fine-grained details but no focus in the first two stages as it lacks global information. It can not focus on the main object until the last stage. (ii) DeiT learns coarse-grained global features over the image but loses details hence difficult to focus on the main content. (iii) Our DaViT captures both short-range and long-range visual dependencies by combining two types of self-attention. It shows strong global modeling capabilities by finding out fine-grained details of the main content in stage 1, and further focusing on some keypoints in stage 2. It then gradually refines the regions of interest from both global and local perspectives for final recognition. Relation to channel-wise attention in CNNs. In Table 1, we summarize the results for baseline models and current state-of- the-art models on the image classification task. We can find our DaViT achieves new state-of-the-art and consistently outperforms other methods with similar model size (#Params.) and computational complexity (GFLOPs). Specifically, DaViT-Tiny, Small, and Base improve over the Swin Transformer [40] by 1.5%, 1.1%, and 1.2%, respectively. Notably, our DaViT-Small with 49.7M parameters reaches 84.2%, which surpasses all counterpart -Base models using much fewer parameters. For example, our DaViT-Small achieves 0.4% and 0.8% higher accu- racy than Focal-Base and Swin-Base, respectively, using near half computations. Following [65,18,40], when 13M images from ImageNet-22k [14] involved for pre-training, DaViT-Base and DaViT-Large obtained 86.9% and 87.5% top-1 accuracy, respectively. Furthermore, when we further scale up DaViT with 1.5B privately collected weakly supervised image-text pairs data and pre-train DaViT with unified contrastive learning [77] approach, DaViT-Huge and DaViT-Gaint reach 90.2% and 90.4% top-1 accuracy on ImageNet with 362M and 1.4B pa- rameters, respectively. 5.2 Object Detection and Instance Segmentation We benchmark our models on object detection with COCO 2017 [38]. We follow the standard to use two training schedules, 1 × schedule with 12 epochs and 3× schedule with 36 epochs. The same multi-scale training strategy as in [40] by randomly resizing the shorter side of the image to the range of [480 , 800] is used. During training, we use AdamW [42] for optimization with initial learning rate 10−4 and weight decay 0.05. We use 0.1, 0.2, and 0.3 stochastic depth drop rates to regularize the training for our tiny, small, and base models, respectively. The numbers of counterparts [40,82] are borrowed from [72]. Results of 1× schedule shown in Table 2 have demonstrated the effectiveness of our method. We observe substantial gains across all settings and metrics compared with several strong transformer baselines. To have more comprehensive comparisons, we further train our models with 3× schedule and show the detailed numbers, #parameters, and associated com- putational costs for RetinaNet and Mask R-CNN in Table 5. As we can see, even for the 3 × schedule, our models can still achieve 1.5-2.9% gains on RetinaNet 3× and 1.0-1.4% gains on Mask R-CNN 3 × over Swin Transformer models. Moreover, from Table 5 we observe a saturated and even degraded mAP in Swin Transformer [40] and Focal Transformer [72] from small to base model, while the mAP of our model is continuously increased with larger model size, showing a better scale-up ability. We benchmark our method on ADE20K [83]. Specifi- cally, we use UperNet [67] as the segmentation method and our DaViT as the backbone. We train three models with DaViT-Tiny, DaViT-Small, DaViT-Base, respectively. For all models, we use a standard recipe by setting the input size to 512 ×512 and train the model for 160k iterations with batch size 16. In Table 4, we show the comparisons to previous works. As we can see, our tiny, small, and base models consistently outperform recent SoTAs, such as 1.2-1.8% gains over Swin Transformers [40] with a similar number of parameters and FLOPs. 5.4 Ablation Study Evaluation of channel group attention at different stages. We make comparisons by inserting a dual attention block at different stages of a window transformer. From the quantitative results in Table 5, we observe: (i) The dual attention module consistently boosts performance at each stage. (ii) Dual atten- tion in the second stage improves the most, as the earlier stage requires more global information. We speculate that the relatively small improvement in the first stage is that local texture features dominate the shallow part of the network. (iii) We achieve the best results when adding dual attention in all four stages. Dual attention layout. We conduct experiments on the layout of our dual at- tention. There are three options with similar computations: (i) window attention first; (ii) channel attention first; and (iii) two types of attention are paralleled ar- ranged. The comparison is shown in Table 6. We can see that the three strategies achieve similar performance, with ‘window attention first’ slightly better. We show that these two self-attentions comple- ment each other: (i) since each channel token contains an abstract representation of the entire image, the channel attention naturally captures global interactions and representations by taking all spatial positions into account when comput- ing attention scores between channels; (ii) spatial attention refines the local representations by performing fine-grained interactions across spatial locations, which in turn helps the global information modeling in channel attention. We further visualize how our channel group attention captures global interactions and demonstrate its effectiveness in various benchmarks. A Appendix A.1 Details of Model Configuration In this work, we follow the design strategy suggested by previous works [40,72]. We divide the entire architecture into four stages, where a patch embedding layer is inserted at the beginning of each stage. Here, our patch embedding layer is implemented by stride convolution. The convolutional kernels and stride values of our four patch embedding layers are {7, 2, 2, 2} and {4, 2, 2, 2}, respectively. Note the large kernel in the first layer introduces almost no additional calcula- tions as the number of input channels is only 3. For the rest kernel values, we use 2 to perform non-overlapping patch merging. We stack our dual attention16 M. Ding et al. Model #Params (M) FLOPs (G) Top-1 (%) DeiT-Tiny [55] 5.7 1.2 72.2 DeiT-Tiny [55] + Channel Attention5.7 1.2 74.9(+2.7) DeiT-Small [55] 22.1 4.5 79.8 DeiT-Small [55] + Channel Attention22.1 4.5 81.2(+1.4) DeiT-Base [55] 86.7 17.4 81.8 DeiT-Base [55] + Channel Attention86.7 17.4 82.3(+0.5) blocks in each stage with the resolution and feature dimension kept the same. These stages jointly produce a hierarchical representation, with the same fea- ture map resolutions as those of typical convolutional networks, e.g., VGG [48] and ResNet [24]. As a result, the proposed architecture can conveniently replace the backbone networks in existing methods for various vision tasks. All model training is accelerated by NVIDIA Apex Automatic Mixed Precision (AMP). Detailed model configurations of our tiny, small, and base models are shown in Table 7. Specifically, take an image with H × W, a C1-dimensional feature with a resolution of H 4 × W 4 is obtained after the first patch embedding layer. And its resolution is further reduced into H 8 × W 8 , H 16 × W 16 , and H 32 × W 32 with the feature dimension increasing to C2, C3, and C4 after the other three patch embedding layer, respectively. For simplicity, we set the window size of 7 × 7 thus Pw = 49 for all models. We also set the number of channels per group Cg = 32 and the number of channels per head Ch = 32 for all blocks of our three models. For DaViT-tiny and DaViT-small, we set the number of heads/groups Nh = Ng = {3, 6, 12, 24} for four stages, respectively; and we set the number of heads/groups Nh = Ng = {4, 8, 16, 32} for four stages in DaViT-base. Also, we set the number of dual attention blocks {1, 1, 3, 1} for our tiny model and {1, 1, 9, 1} for the small and base models. For the models without FFN, we simply change the number of dual attention blocks to keep the total computation costs similar, though we believe there should be a better configuration specifically for them. We set the number of dual attention blocks {2, 2, 11, 2} for our tiny model (without FFN) and {2, 2, 28, 2} for the small and base models (without FFN). When more training data is involved, we further scale up DaViT to large, huge, and giant sizes to validate the scaling ability of the proposed architec- ture for image classification with larger input resolutions. Considering larger image resolutions and model sizes used, we suspect that the dot products of self-attention grow large in magnitude in this case, as in [59]. To counteract this effect, we scale the dot products in our channel attention by 1√ P . We set C1 = {192, 256, 384}, N1 h = N1 g = {6, 8, 12}, and the number of dual atten- tion blocks of the third stage as {9, 9, 12} respectively for large, huge, and giant models.DaViT: Dual Attention Vision Transformers 17 Table 9.Comparison of Transformers without FFNs on ImageNet-1K. We alter- natively arrange the vanilla patch-level self-attention and our channel group self-attention. We set the number of groups and channels of our channel-wise group attention the same as the number of heads and channels of self-attention in DeiT, to make the number of parameters and FLOPs comparable with the vanilla DeiT. From Table 8 we observe substantial gains of our dual attention across all model sizes, e.g., 2.7% over the tiny model and 0.5% even compared to the base model. The result shows that our channel-wise attention can be com- bined with window attention and patch-level global attention, improving the performance of both spatial-wise self-attentions. A.3 Transformer without FFNs FFN has been a default component of Transformers with little research on it. However, it dominates the number of FLOPs and model parameters of our DaViT. Considering our dual attention has both channel-wise and spatial-wise interactions, we conduct an initial exploration to show the potential of the pure- attention structure without FFNs. We remove FFNs and add more dual attention blocks to match the computational costs. From Table 9 we see that: pure dual attention without FFNs achieves 1.5% and 1.7% better Top-1 accuracy than pure window attention and channel at- tention, respectively, showing the effectiveness of dual attention that has both channel-wise and spatial-wise interactions. The model without FFN shows com- parable and even better performance with models like PVT [63] and DeiT [55], but still inferior to recent SoTAs like Swin [40], Focal [72], and our full DaViT. A.4 Model Capacity against Model Depth Our DaViT contains both spatial-wise and channel-wise attention, and both lo- cal and global interactions in each dual attention block. We conduct experiments to show whether it needs less number of layers to obtain similar modeling capac- ity as previous works [40,72]. A.5 Throughput Analysis In addition to the main criteria of computational cost in the main paper, i.e., FLOPs and #parameters, we report the real-time inference speed/throughput against Swin Transformer [40] to show the efficiency of our work. Compared to the strong baseline Swin, our model does have advantages in real-time through- put as the cleaner structure and high efficiency of the group channel atten- tion. For example, we remove the shifted window partition, which depends on torch.roll() to perform cyclic shift and its reverse on features. This operation is not fully optimized by popular inference frameworks. Table 11 demonstrates the comparison between Swin and DaViT. Nvidia V100 GPU is utilized for the benchmark, and the image resolution is 224 × 224. It shows that DaViT consis- tently outperforms Swin across different model sizes in efficiency.DaViT: Dual Attention Vision Transformers 19 A.6 Comparisons with SE and ECA Blocks To make quantitative comparisons with traditional channel-wise attentions blocks, we did experiments by replacing the channel self-attention in our tiny model with SE block [28] and ECA block [61]. The results in Table 12 show that our chan- nel group self-attention is more powerful by performing dynamic feature fusion across global tokens (different global views of the entire image) in transformers. It shows superior performance (1.6%) than both of the two variants using SE [28] and ECA [61] blocks, respectively. References 1. new_method.method:
タイトル: Rev-SS2D — Reversible, Streaming, and Shared-State Vision Mamba（Vision Mambaの学習メモリを本質的に削減する統合設計）

Motivation
- 何が問題か: VMamba/Vimの計算は線形だが、学習ピークメモリは依然として高く、2K–4K解像での単GPU学習は困難。主因は(1)多段VSSブロックの活性保持、(2)四方向SS2Dでの重複活性・状態、(3)チェックポイント後も残る境界活性・正規化統計。(4)大解像ではI/O圧が強く、チェックポイント再計算のオーバーヘッドが隠しにくい。
- 既存ギャップ: 先行のVMamba/Vimは四方向走査を採用するが、方向間で重み/状態を共有せず、可逆学習や2Dストリーミング後伝播を統合していない。Mamba-2/SSDは1D最適化中心で、2Dタイル単位の状態受け渡し・逆伝播カーネルは未整備。StableSSMの“best”再パラメタや低精度（FP8/INT8）状態の訓練時活用も未検証。
- なぜ今か: 超高解像タスク（病理・衛星・地図）やオンデバイス学習のニーズ増。FlashAttention型の流式ADや可逆ネットの成熟、FP8/INT8ハードの普及により、SS2Dでも原理的にピークメモリを削れる統合設計が現実的になった。

Methodology
1) 可逆VSSブロック（Rev-SS2D）
- 可逆結合: 入力X=[X1,X2]に対しY1=X1+α·G1(SS2D(RevLN(X2)))、Y2=X2+α·G2(SS2D(RevLN(Y1)))。
  - αはsoftplus係数またはスペクトル正則化付与でLipschitz<1を維持。初期化はα≪1で安定化。
  - Gは1×1線形+SiLUの軽量ゲート。線形/1×1はスペクトルノルム拘束。
- RevLN/RMSNorm: タイル単位の(μ,σ)のみ保存し、逆向きで厳密に復元可能な正規化（出力y=(x−μ)/σ·γ+β、逆はx=y·σ/γ+μ−β·σ/γ）。ドロップアウト/DropPathはRNG seedのみ保持。
- 逆伝播: 前活性を非保存。保存するのは(Y1,Y2)と各タイルごとの(μ,σ)、RNG seedのみ。Backwardでは(X1,X2)を逐次復元→SS2D/DWConv/Linearを再計算。勾配クリップとαスケジュールで安定化。
- 差分点: ViT/ResNet系のRevNetはあるが、SS2Dと四方向走査を含むVSSブロック全体を可逆化するのは新規。

2) 方向共有ステート（Shared-State SS2D）
- 重み共有: 四方向(LR/RL/TB/BT)でSSMパラメタ(A,B,C,dt,D)を共有。方向固有性は軽量アダプタで付与。
  - OrderScale: 方向ごとのチャネル別Affine(γ,β)。
  - LoRA-Δ: 方向ごとにB/Cに低ランク補正（r∈{0,2,4}）。
- 実行共有: 学習時は単一SSMインスタンスと単一状態バッファを方向に合わせて順次使い回す。方向切替は(γ,β)/LoRAのみ切替。
- 正則化: 方向アダプタはゼロ中心初期化+weight decay。共有重みにはEMAを導入し学習安定化。
- 差分点: VMamba/Vimは方向独立。方向共有で状態・活性の重複を除去する設計は未報告。

3) SS2D用2Dタイル・ストリーミング自動微分（Streaming-SS2D）
- タイル化: 特徴をT_h×T_wタイル（例64×64）で分割。四方向走査に対しタイル順走査→隣接タイルへ状態ハンドオフ。
- 最小保存: 保存は「タイル境界SSM状態」「RevLN(μ,σ)」「RNG seed」「方向アダプタ」のみ。DWConv/Linear/BC等は後伝播で再計算。
- ハロー: DWConv受容野に応じて(k−1)のハローを参照。ハローも再計算可能なため保存不要。
- カーネル: Mamba-2のSSD fused pathを拡張し、2Dタイル・四方向・ハロー・varlenを扱うTritonカーネルを実装。custom autogradで前向き=タイルストリーム、後向き=可逆復元→タイル再計算→勾配蓄積。
- 差分点: 1D SSDのchunk/state passingをSS2Dへ拡張し、可逆/共有/量子化まで含めて一体化するのが新規。

4) 安定・低精度状態（Stable+Quantized States）
- StableSSM“best”再パラメタ: 離散A=1−1/(a w²+b)、dt=softplus(·)をSS2Dに導入（従来のexpマップとハイブリッド）。勾配スケールを均衡化し再計算/量子化に強い学習を実現。
- 量子化状態: ssm_state/conv_state/xBCをFP8(E4M3/E5M2)またはINT8で保持。per-head/per-groupスケール+誤差フィードバック（QSGD風）で量子化誤差を抑制。勾配はFP16/32で集計。
- 安定化: Lipschitz(α/スペクトルノルム)、dt制約、RMSNorm/RevLN併用、勾配クリップ/EMAで数値安定。
- 差分点: 視覚SS2Dでの“best”再パラメタ+学習時状態量子化の組合せは未検証。

5) 実装互換性・最小改変
- API: VSSBlockにrev=TrueでRev-SS2Dへ切替。LN→RevLN置換。SS2Dにdirection_idと共有SSM/アダプタを追加。
- カーネル: Cross-Scan/Merge/SSDをタイル/状態受け渡し/varlen対応へ拡張。フォールバックで標準チェックポイント路も選択可。
- 推論: 逆伝播不要のため共有/タイル化は任意。Mamba-2 SSDの高速路は維持。

6) 複雑度・メモリ見積と検証
- ピークメモリ: 既存 ~O(B·H·W·C) → 提案 ~O(B·C·T_h·T_w)+O(B·nheads·headdim·d_state)+O(#tilesの小統計)。方向共有で状態ピーク≈1/4。期待削減2–5×。
- 再計算オーバーヘッド: +5–15%程度（タイル再計算・可逆復元分）。大解像ではI/O隠蔽のため実効スループット低下は限定的。
- 正しさ検証: saved-activation版との数値差分チェック（grad相対誤差<1e-3目標）、RNGシード固定で再現性確認。

7) 実験計画（再現性重視）
- ベースライン: VMamba-{T/S/B}標準学習、勾配チェックポイント、IO-aware実装。
- 設定: 単GPU24–40GB、AMP(fp16/bf16)、Tile={32,64,96}、FP8/INT8状態有無。
- タスク:
  - ImageNet-1K 224→長辺768線形チューニング。
  - ADE20K UperNet: crop 512/1024、マルチスケール。
  - COCO Mask R-CNN: 長辺1280/1500、3×MS。
  - 高解像系: DeepGlobe/SpaceNet/Pathology（2K–4Kクロップ）。
- 指標: Peakメモリ（torch.cuda.max_memory_allocated）、実効バッチ、吞吐、精度（Top-1/mIoU/AP）、学習安定性（loss NaN率）。
- アブレーション:
  - 可逆off/方向共有off/ストリーミングoff/量子化offの寄与。
  - LoRAランクr∈{0,2,4}、OrderScaleのみ比較。
  - StableSSM“best” vs exp、αスケジュール、タイルサイズ。
  - 状態精度: FP16 vs FP8(E4M3/E5M2)/INT8、誤差フィードバック有無。

Expected impact
- メモリ効率: VMamba比で2–4×削減、2K–4Kで>5×を目標。24–40GB GPUで勾配蓄積なしの実用バッチを実現。
- 精度/安定性: 方向アダプタ（LoRA/OrderScale）で四方向独立と同等の表現力を維持、Top-1/下流性能で±0.2%以内を目標。StableSSM“best”とRevLNで低精度状態でも収束安定。
- 汎用性: 既存VMamba/Vimコードへの薄い改変で適用可能。動画（時空タイル）、3Dボリューム、マルチモーダル格子へ自然拡張。可逆×ストリーミング設計はConv/Attention混成ブロックにも転用可。
- 実運用: 医療・衛星・計算病理・地図など超高解像学習のボトルネックを大幅に緩和。オンデバイス/省電力学習の可能性拡大。クラウドコスト削減と学習再現性向上（保存テンソル削減によるチェックポイント軽量化）。
- 新規性（先行との差分）: VMamba/Vimが未採用の(1)可逆VSSブロック、(2)四方向の重み/状態共有、(3)2Dタイル流式オートグラド、(4)StableSSM“best”＋学習時状態量子化の統合。Mamba-2/SSDの1D手法をSS2Dに拡張し、四方向クロススキャン・可逆結合・方向アダプタ・量子化状態を一体で扱う点が新規。

experimental_design.experiment_strategy:
Title: Experiments to validate Rev-SS2D — Reversible, Streaming, and Shared-State Vision Mamba

Overview
- We propose three complementary experiments that are feasible to implement in Python and highlight memory savings, correctness, scalability to high resolution, and the representational impact of direction sharing and quantized states.
- Each experiment includes: goals, setup, procedure, metrics, success criteria, and runnable code or commands.

============================================================
Experiment 1 — Unit correctness and memory/speed microbench (synthetic + ImageNet-mini)
============================================================
Goal
- Demonstrate that Rev-SS2D (reversible + streaming + shared-state) reduces peak training memory with minimal overhead and maintains correct gradients vs a saved-activations baseline.
- Validate correctness via grad-difference tests (<1e-3 relative error target) and RNG reproducibility. Quantify memory reduction and throughput overhead across tile sizes and state precisions (FP16 vs FP8/INT8 simulated).

Setup
- Single GPU (24–40 GB), PyTorch ≥ 2.1. Triton optional (the code provides a pure-PyTorch fallback). AMP bf16/fp16.
- Synthetic tensors at resolutions up to 2K–4K to stress memory. Optionally ImageNet-mini (10 classes, ~13k imgs) for brief sanity training.
- Baselines: vanilla VSS block (no reversibility, no streaming, independent directions). Variants: +rev, +stream, +share, +quant, and full Rev-SS2D.

Procedure
1) Build minimal SS2D operator with four directional scans, a reversible VSS block (Rev-SS2D), shared-state adapters, and a streaming-tile executor that passes boundary states. Provide a quantized state buffer (FP8/INT8 simulated) for tile boundary storage.
2) Microbench forward+backward with random inputs at resolutions: 512, 1536, 3072 (square or long-side) and batch sizes 1–4, logging peak memory, step time, and success (no OOM).
3) Gradient correctness: compare model grads vs saved-activation baseline with identical seeds (relative L2 error target <1e-3).
4) Ablations: toggle rev/share/stream/quant and sweep tile sizes {32, 64, 96}; state precision {fp16 (baseline), fp8(E4M3) sim, int8}.

Metrics
- torch.cuda.max_memory_allocated(), images/sec or tokens/sec, grad relative error, loss NaN rate.

Success criteria
- Memory reduction ≥2× at 1K–2K inputs (full combo vs baseline); ≥4–5× at 2K–4K vs activation-saving baseline.
- Throughput overhead ≤15% at ≥1K resolution.
- Grad relative error <1e-3, identical RNG reproducibility.

Code (pure-PyTorch runnable skeleton)
- Notes: This is a compact, functional demonstration. It favors clarity over speed and uses naive tensor ops for SS2D scans. Triton/optimized kernels can replace the slow parts without changing the experiment API. The quantization here is simulated/fake-quant for training memory benefit at tile boundaries.

```python
import math, time, os, random
from dataclasses import dataclass
from typing import Optional, Tuple
import torch
import torch.nn as nn
import torch.nn.functional as F
from torch.utils.data import DataLoader

# ------------------------
# Utilities
# ------------------------

def set_seed(seed=42):
    random.seed(seed)
    torch.manual_seed(seed)
    torch.cuda.manual_seed_all(seed)
    torch.backends.cudnn.deterministic = True
    torch.backends.cudnn.benchmark = False

class EMA:
    def __init__(self, model, decay=0.999):
        self.decay = decay
        self.shadow = {}
        for n, p in model.named_parameters():
            if p.requires_grad:
                self.shadow[n] = p.detach().clone()
    @torch.no_grad()
    def update(self, model):
        for n, p in model.named_parameters():
            if p.requires_grad:
                self.shadow[n].mul_(self.decay).add_(p.detach(), alpha=1-self.decay)

# ------------------------
# Reversible LayerNorm (RevLN): stores only (mu, sigma) per tile
# ------------------------
class RevLN(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self, dim, eps=1e-5):
        super().__init__()
        self.gamma = nn.Parameter(torch.ones(dim))
        self.beta = nn.Parameter(torch.zeros(dim))
        self.eps = eps
    def forward(self, x):
        # x: (B,C,H,W)
        mu = x.mean(dim=(2,3), keepdim=True)
        var = x.var(dim=(2,3), unbiased=False, keepdim=True)
        sigma = (var + self.eps).sqrt()
        y = (x - mu) / sigma
        y = y * self.gamma.view(1,-1,1,1) + self.beta.view(1,-1,1,1)
        # return normalized + stats for reversible inverse in backward
        return y, (mu.detach(), sigma.detach())
    def inverse(self, y, stats):
        mu, sigma = stats
        x = (y - self.beta.view(1,-1,1,1)) / self.gamma.view(1,-1,1,1)
        x = x * sigma + mu
        return x

# ------------------------
# Minimal SS2D with shared state across directions
#   - Four directional scans using a simple linear recurrence per row/col
#   - Shared parameters; direction-specific adapters (OrderScale + optional LoRA)
# ------------------------
@dataclass
class SS2DConfig:
    channels: int
    d_state: int = 8
    lora_rank: int = 0  # 0,2,4

class SharedStateSS2D(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self, cfg: SS2DConfig):
        super().__init__()
        C, D = cfg.channels, cfg.d_state
        # StableSSM-like reparam: A in (0,1) via 1 - 1/(a*w^2+b) simplified surrogate
        self.a = nn.Parameter(torch.ones(C, D) * 0.5)
        self.b = nn.Parameter(torch.ones(C, D) * 1.0)
        self.dt_raw = nn.Parameter(torch.zeros(C, D))
        # Input/state/output projections (1x1 conv-as-linear for (B,C,H,W))
        self.in_proj = nn.Conv2d(C, C, 1)
        self.out_proj = nn.Conv2d(C, C, 1)
        # Direction adapters: per-direction affine
        self.dir_gamma = nn.ParameterDict({d: nn.Parameter(torch.ones(C)) for d in ["lr","rl","tb","bt"]})
        self.dir_beta  = nn.ParameterDict({d: nn.Parameter(torch.zeros(C)) for d in ["lr","rl","tb","bt"]})
        # Optional LoRA-style low-rank adapters for input/output paths
        self.lora_rank = cfg.lora_rank
        if self.lora_rank > 0:
            r = self.lora_rank
            self.lora_in_A  = nn.ParameterDict({d: nn.Parameter(torch.zeros(C, r)) for d in ["lr","rl","tb","bt"]})
            self.lora_in_B  = nn.ParameterDict({d: nn.Parameter(torch.zeros(r, C)) for d in ["lr","rl","tb","bt"]})
            self.lora_out_A = nn.ParameterDict({d: nn.Parameter(torch.zeros(C, r)) for d in ["lr","rl","tb","bt"]})
            self.lora_out_B = nn.ParameterDict({d: nn.Parameter(torch.zeros(r, C)) for d in ["lr","rl","tb","bt"]})
        else:
            self.lora_in_A = self.lora_in_B = self.lora_out_A = self.lora_out_B = None
        # Spectral/Lipschitz control would be applied to in_proj/out_proj in a real system
    def _A(self):
        # stable A in (0,1) proxy; for demo keep it simple and bounded
        return torch.sigmoid(self.a) * 0.99
    def _dt(self):
        return F.softplus(self.dt_raw) + 1e-3
    def _apply_dir_adapt(self, x, d):
        g = self.dir_gamma[d].view(1,-1,1,1)
        b = self.dir_beta[d].view(1,-1,1,1)
        x = x * g + b
        if self.lora_rank > 0:
            # (B,C,H,W) -> LoRA apply as 1x1 linear delta
            B,C,H,W = x.shape
            x_flat = x.permute(0,2,3,1).reshape(-1, C)
            x_flat = x_flat + x_flat @ self.lora_in_A[d] @ self.lora_in_B[d]
            x = x_flat.view(B,H,W,C).permute(0,3,1,2)
        return x
    def _apply_dir_out(self, y, d):
        if self.lora_rank > 0:
            B,C,H,W = y.shape
            y_flat = y.permute(0,2,3,1).reshape(-1, C)
            y_flat = y_flat + y_flat @ self.lora_out_A[d] @ self.lora_out_B[d]
            y = y_flat.view(B,H,W,C).permute(0,3,1,2)
        return y
    @torch.no_grad()
    def _scan1d_(self, x, A, dt, dim):
        # x: (B,C,H,W). dim=3 for width, dim=2 for height
        # naive python loop for clarity; replace with Triton for speed
        if dim == 3:  # along width
            B,C,H,W = x.shape
            y = torch.zeros_like(x)
            state = torch.zeros(B, C, H, self._A().shape[1], device=x.device, dtype=x.dtype)
            for i in range(W):
                xi = x[:,:,:,i]
                state = A*state + dt.unsqueeze(0).unsqueeze(2)*xi.unsqueeze(-1)
                yi = state.mean(dim=-1)  # simple readout
                y[:,:,:,i] = yi
            return y
        else:  # along height
            B,C,H,W = x.shape
            y = torch.zeros_like(x)
            state = torch.zeros(B, C, W, self._A().shape[1], device=x.device, dtype=x.dtype)
            for i in range(H):
                xi = x[:,:,i,:]
                state = A*state + dt.unsqueeze(0).unsqueeze(2)*xi.unsqueeze(-1)
                yi = state.mean(dim=-1)
                y[:,:,i,:] = yi
            return y
    def forward(self, x):
        # Shared SSM with per-direction adapters
        A = self._A(); dt = self._dt()
        # In-proj shared
        h = self.in_proj(x)
        outs = []
        for d, dim in [("lr",3),("rl",3),("tb",2),("bt",2)]:
            hin = self._apply_dir_adapt(h, d)
            if d == "lr":
                y = self._scan1d_(hin, A, dt, dim=3)
            elif d == "rl":
                y = torch.flip(self._scan1d_(torch.flip(hin, dims=[3]), A, dt, dim=3), dims=[3])
            elif d == "tb":
                y = self._scan1d_(hin, A, dt, dim=2)
            else:  # bt
                y = torch.flip(self._scan1d_(torch.flip(hin, dims=[2]), A, dt, dim=2), dims=[2])
            y = self._apply_dir_out(y, d)
            outs.append(y)
        y = sum(outs) / 4.0
        y = self.out_proj(y)
        return y

# ------------------------
# Quantized state buffer for tile boundary handoff (fake-quant FP8/INT8)
# ------------------------
class QuantBuf:
    def __init__(self, mode="none", group_size=32, eps=1e-8):
        assert mode in ["none","fp8","int8"]
        self.mode = mode
        self.group_size = group_size
        self.eps = eps
    def quantize(self, x):
        if self.mode == "none":
            return x, None
        B = x.shape[0]
        x_flat = x.view(B, -1)
        G = self.group_size
        n_groups = (x_flat.shape[1] + G - 1)//G
        padsz = n_groups*G - x_flat.shape[1]
        if padsz > 0:
            x_flat = F.pad(x_flat, (0,padsz))
        x_groups = x_flat.view(B, n_groups, G)
        scale = x_groups.abs().amax(dim=2, keepdim=True).clamp(min=self.eps) / (127.0 if self.mode=="int8" else 240.0)
        q = torch.round(x_groups/scale).clamp(-128,127) if self.mode=="int8" else torch.round(x_groups/scale).clamp(-240,240)
        q = q.to(torch.int16)  # store compactly; could pack further
        return (q, scale), (x.shape, padsz)
    def dequantize(self, packed):
        if self.mode == "none":
            return packed
        (q, scale), meta = packed
        shape, padsz = meta
        x_groups = (q.float()*scale).view(shape[0], -1)
        if padsz>0:
            x_groups = x_groups[:,:-padsz]
        return x_groups.view(*shape)

# ------------------------
# Reversible VSS block (Rev-SS2D)
# Y1 = X1 + α G1(SS2D(RevLN(X2)))
# Y2 = X2 + α G2(SS2D(RevLN(Y1)))
# ------------------------
class Gate(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self, C):
        super().__init__()
        self.lin = nn.Conv2d(C, C, 1)
        self.act = nn.SiLU()
    def forward(self, x):
        return self.act(self.lin(x))

class RevSS2DFunction(torch.autograd.Function):
    @staticmethod
    def forward(ctx, x1, x2, ss2d: SharedStateSS2D, ln1: RevLN, ln2: RevLN, g1: Gate, g2: Gate, alpha: float):
        with torch.no_grad():
            y1_norm, stats1 = ln1(x2)
            y1 = x1 + alpha * g1(ss2d(y1_norm))
            y2_norm, stats2 = ln2(y1)
            y2 = x2 + alpha * g2(ss2d(y2_norm))
        # Save minimal info; we keep (y1,y2) and LN stats; recompute inside backward
        ctx.ss2d = ss2d; ctx.ln1 = ln1; ctx.ln2 = ln2; ctx.g1 = g1; ctx.g2 = g2; ctx.alpha = alpha
        ctx.save_for_backward(y1, y2, stats1[0], stats1[1], stats2[0], stats2[1])
        return y1, y2
    @staticmethod
    def backward(ctx, dy1, dy2):
        ss2d, ln1, ln2, g1, g2, alpha = ctx.ss2d, ctx.ln1, ctx.ln2, ctx.g1, ctx.g2, ctx.alpha
        y1, y2, mu1, sig1, mu2, sig2 = ctx.saved_tensors
        # Reconstruct x2, x1
        with torch.enable_grad():
            y2 = y2.detach().requires_grad_(True)
            y1 = y1.detach().requires_grad_(True)
            # x2 = y2 - alpha * g2(SS2D(RevLN(y1)))
            y1_norm = (y1 - ln2.beta.view(1,-1,1,1)) / ln2.gamma.view(1,-1,1,1)
            y1_norm = y1_norm * sig2 + mu2
            z2 = g2(ss2d(y1_norm))
            x2 = y2 - alpha * z2
            # x1 = y1 - alpha * g1(SS2D(RevLN(x2)))
            x2_norm = (x2 - ln1.beta.view(1,-1,1,1)) / ln1.gamma.view(1,-1,1,1)
            x2_norm = x2_norm * sig1 + mu1
            z1 = g1(ss2d(x2_norm))
            x1 = y1 - alpha * z1
            # Now compute gradients via autograd of reconstructed graph
            grads = torch.autograd.grad(outputs=(y1, y2),
                                        inputs=(x1, x2) + tuple(ss2d.parameters()) + tuple(ln1.parameters()) + tuple(ln2.parameters()) + tuple(g1.parameters()) + tuple(g2.parameters()),
                                        grad_outputs=(dy1, dy2),
                                        allow_unused=True, retain_graph=False)
        # Pull out grads for x1, x2 and module params
        gx1, gx2, *gparams = grads
        # Accumulate param grads manually
        offset = 0
        def add_grads(module):
            nonlocal offset
            for p in module.parameters():
                if p.requires_grad:
                    gp = gparams[offset]
                    offset += 1
                    if gp is not None:
                        if p.grad is None:
                            p.grad = gp.detach()
                        else:
                            p.grad.add_(gp.detach())
        add_grads(ss2d); add_grads(ln1); add_grads(ln2); add_grads(g1); add_grads(g2)
        return gx1, gx2, None, None, None, None, None, None

class RevSS2DBlock(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self, C, d_state=8, lora_rank=0, alpha=0.2):
        super().__init__()
        self.ss2d = SharedStateSS2D(SS2DConfig(C, d_state, lora_rank))
        self.ln1 = RevLN(C); self.ln2 = RevLN(C)
        self.g1 = Gate(C); self.g2 = Gate(C)
        self.alpha = alpha
    def forward(self, x):
        # x: (B,C,H,W); split channels into two partitions for reversibility
        C = x.shape[1]
        x1, x2 = torch.chunk(x, 2, dim=1)
        y1, y2 = RevSS2DFunction.apply(x1, x2, self.ss2d, self.ln1, self.ln2, self.g1, self.g2, self.alpha)
        return torch.cat([y1, y2], dim=1)

# ------------------------
# Streaming tile executor (naive) to show state handoff and quantized boundary storage
# ------------------------
class StreamingSS2DWrapper(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self, block: RevSS2DBlock, tile: int = 64, quant_mode="none"):
        super().__init__()
        self.block = block
        self.tile = tile
        self.q = QuantBuf(mode=quant_mode)
    def forward(self, x):
        B,C,H,W = x.shape
        Th = Tw = self.tile
        # pad to tile multiple
        pad_h = (Th - H % Th) % Th
        pad_w = (Tw - W % Tw) % Tw
        x = F.pad(x, (0,pad_w,0,pad_h))
        H2, W2 = x.shape[2], x.shape[3]
        # naive tile loop; no true halo here (dwconv omitted for brevity)
        y = torch.zeros_like(x)
        # simple per-row state buffer demo (quantized) — placeholder
        row_state = torch.zeros(B, C//2, self.block.ss2d._A().shape[1], device=x.device, dtype=x.dtype)
        row_state_pack, meta = self.q.quantize(row_state)
        for i in range(0, H2, Th):
            for j in range(0, W2, Tw):
                tile_x = x[:, :, i:i+Th, j:j+Tw]
                # dequantize state (demo)
                _ = self.q.dequantize((row_state_pack, meta))
                y[:, :, i:i+Th, j:j+Tw] = self.block(tile_x)
        return y[:, :, :H, :W]

# ------------------------
# Baseline block for comparison (no reversibility, no sharing), using independent SS2D per direction
# ------------------------
class IndepSS2DBlock(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self, C, d_state=8, alpha=0.2):
        super().__init__()
        self.ss_lr = SharedStateSS2D(SS2DConfig(C, d_state))
        self.ss_rl = SharedStateSS2D(SS2DConfig(C, d_state))
        self.ss_tb = SharedStateSS2D(SS2DConfig(C, d_state))
        self.ss_bt = SharedStateSS2D(SS2DConfig(C, d_state))
        self.ln1 = nn.LayerNorm(C)
        self.g = nn.Sequential(nn.Conv2d(C, C, 1), nn.SiLU())
        self.alpha = alpha
    def forward(self, x):
        # For fair comparison we apply 4 scans and merge
        N,C,H,W = x.shape
        x_nhwc = x.permute(0,2,3,1)
        x_ln = self.ln1(x_nhwc).permute(0,3,1,2).contiguous()
        outs = [self.ss_lr(x_ln), self.ss_rl(x_ln), self.ss_tb(x_ln), self.ss_bt(x_ln)]
        y = sum(outs)/4.0
        return x + self.alpha * self.g(y)

# ------------------------
# Microbench helpers
# ------------------------

def peak_mem_mb():
    return torch.cuda.max_memory_allocated() / (1024**2)

def bench_step(model, shape=(1,96,1024,1024), amp_dtype=torch.bfloat16, steps=10):
    device = torch.device('cuda' if torch.cuda.is_available() else 'cpu')
    model = model.to(device)
    opt = torch.optim.AdamW(model.parameters(), lr=1e-3)
    scaler = torch.cuda.amp.GradScaler(enabled=(amp_dtype==torch.float16))
    torch.cuda.reset_peak_memory_stats(device)
    x = torch.randn(shape, device=device)
    t0 = time.time()
    for s in range(steps):
        opt.zero_grad(set_to_none=True)
        with torch.autocast(device_type='cuda', dtype=amp_dtype):
            y = model(x)
            loss = y.square().mean()
        loss.backward()
        opt.step()
    torch.cuda.synchronize()
    t1 = time.time()
    return {
        'peak_mem_mb': peak_mem_mb(),
        'step_time_s': (t1-t0)/steps,
        'ok': True
    }

def grad_check_relative_error(model_a, model_b, shape=(2,96,256,256), amp_dtype=torch.float32):
    device = torch.device('cuda' if torch.cuda.is_available() else 'cpu')
    set_seed(123)
    ma = model_a.to(device); mb = model_b.to(device)
    for m in [ma, mb]:
        for p in m.parameters():
            if p.requires_grad:
                nn.init.normal_(p, std=0.02)
    x = torch.randn(shape, device=device, requires_grad=True)
    def run(m):
        for p in m.parameters():
            if p.grad is not None: p.grad.zero_()
        with torch.autocast(device_type='cuda', dtype=amp_dtype):
            y = m(x)
            loss = (y**2).mean()
        loss.backward()
        grads = torch.cat([p.grad.flatten() for p in m.parameters() if p.grad is not None])
        return grads
    ga = run(ma).detach().cpu()
    set_seed(123)
    gb = run(mb).detach().cpu()
    rel_err = (ga - gb).norm() / (ga.norm() + 1e-8)
    return rel_err.item()

if __name__ == "__main__":
    set_seed(42)
    device = torch.device('cuda' if torch.cuda.is_available() else 'cpu')

    C = 96
    base = IndepSS2DBlock(C=C, d_state=8, alpha=0.2)
    rev_block = RevSS2DBlock(C=C, d_state=8, lora_rank=2, alpha=0.2)
    rev_stream = StreamingSS2DWrapper(rev_block, tile=64, quant_mode="fp8")

    print("Grad correctness vs baseline (small shape)...")
    rel = grad_check_relative_error(base, rev_block, shape=(2,C,128,128), amp_dtype=torch.float32)
    print("rel_error:", rel)

    print("Microbench baseline 1024^2 ...")
    print(bench_step(base, shape=(1,C,1024,1024), amp_dtype=torch.bfloat16, steps=5))

    print("Microbench Rev-SS2D (no streaming) 1024^2 ...")
    print(bench_step(rev_block, shape=(1,C,1024,1024), amp_dtype=torch.bfloat16, steps=5))

    print("Microbench Rev-SS2D + streaming(fp8) 1536^2 ...")
    print(bench_step(rev_stream, shape=(1,C,1536,1536), amp_dtype=torch.bfloat16, steps=3))

    # Try a 2K or 3K run if memory allows
    try:
        print("Rev-SS2D + streaming(fp8) 2048^2 ...")
        print(bench_step(rev_stream, shape=(1,C,2048,2048), amp_dtype=torch.bfloat16, steps=2))
    except RuntimeError as e:
        print("OOM or error:", e)
```

How to run
- python rev_ss2d_microbench.py
- Adjust C, d_state, tile, quant_mode, and shapes; observe peak_mem_mb and step_time_s.

Interpretation
- Expect rel_error < 1e-3. Baseline may OOM at ≥1536^2 while Rev-SS2D + streaming(fp8) should run with 2–4× lower peak memory. Overhead per step ≤15% at large shapes.


============================================================
Experiment 2 — High-resolution semantic segmentation on ADE20K (512/1024 crops)
============================================================
Goal
- Show Rev-SS2D enables higher resolution and/or larger effective batch sizes on a single 24–40 GB GPU with competitive mIoU vs a VMamba baseline.
- Evaluate stability with low-precision state buffers (FP8 simulated), and the impact of tile size on speed/accuracy.

Setup
- Dataset: ADE20K (training 20K, val 2K). Use standard train/val split.
- Model: UPerNet with a VMamba-S style backbone. Replace VSS blocks in backbone stages with RevSS2DBlock and a streaming wrapper. Direction shared-state on; LoRA rank r ∈ {0,2,4}.
- Hardware: 1× A6000/RTX 6000 Ada/3090/4090 (24–48 GB).
- AMP: bf16 or fp16.

Procedure
1) Integrate Rev-SS2D module into the backbone (timm-like or mmsegmentation). If using MMSegmentation:
   - Implement a Backbone class RevVSSBackbone that selects RevSS2DBlock when cfg.rev=True.
   - Expose tile size and quant_mode to the config.
2) Train configurations:
   - Baseline VMamba-S: crop=512, batch=16 (accumulate if needed), no streaming, independent directions.
   - Rev-SS2D: crop=1024, batch as large as fits (target ≥8 on 24–40 GB), streaming tile=64, quant_mode ∈ {none, fp8}.
   - Optional: 3rd run Rev-SS2D + FP8 states + EMA on shared weights.
3) Keep standard UPerNet training settings: AdamW, lr=6e-4, wd=0.05, 160k iters, cosine decay, poly LR acceptable; multi-scale eval on val.
4) Log: peak memory, imgs/sec, loss NaN rate, mIoU.

Metrics
- mIoU (single-scale and multi-scale), peak memory, throughput, convergence time.

Success criteria
- At crop=1024, Rev-SS2D fits ≥1.5–2× larger batch than baseline on same GPU or supports higher crop without OOM.
- mIoU within ±0.2 of baseline at matched compute, with stable training under FP8 state mode.
